question: how to make compost tea at home
title: How to Make Compost Tea at Home (The Easy Version That Actually Works)
metaDescription: Compost tea is made by bubbling water through finished compost for 24 hours. Here's the simple home method, what it does for your soil and plants, and the one mistake that ruins most batches.
content:
# How to Make Compost Tea at Home (The Easy Version That Actually Works)

I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book. The book described elaborate setups with specialized pumps, precise ratios, temperature controls, and specific compost sources. I tried a simplified version of that setup and was skeptical. Then I watched what happened in my garden over the following season, and I became a believer.

Compost tea is one of those things that sounds more complicated than it is. The basic concept is simple: you're taking the living organisms from finished compost and multiplying them in water, then applying that water to your soil or plant leaves. You're not creating nutrients — you're creating biology. And there's a meaningful difference between those two things.

## What Compost Tea Is (and Isn't)

Compost tea is not liquid fertilizer. I want to be clear about that upfront, because people sometimes expect it to perform like a fertilizer and are puzzled when it doesn't produce a big green flush of growth. That's not what it's for.

What compost tea does is inoculate your soil with microbial life. The bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and nematodes that live in your finished compost get suspended in water and proliferated through aeration. When you apply that water to your soil, you're essentially broadcasting beneficial microorganisms across a wide area — far more coverage than you'd get by physically spreading the same amount of compost.

University of Minnesota Extension's resources on living soil describe compost as containing the microorganisms and organic matter that improve soil by providing bacteria, fungi, and other organisms with nutrients, energy, and habitat. Compost tea takes those organisms and extends their reach.

Cornell University's compost research notes that compost showed up to 47 percent disease suppression compared to non-enriched compost in plant pathogen studies. The biology in compost — and by extension, in well-made compost tea — actively competes with and suppresses pathogens that might otherwise harm your plants.

## The Basic Method: What You Need

You don't need much. Here's what makes a functional home compost tea setup:

**Finished compost.** This is the most important ingredient. It needs to be good compost — dark, earthy-smelling, fully decomposed, teeming with life. Immature compost or compost that never heated properly won't give you the biology you're looking for. If you're not sure if your compost is ready, do the smell test: it should smell like forest floor, not like something decomposing.

**Unchlorinated water.** This is the step most people miss. Chlorine in municipal tap water is there specifically to kill bacteria. If you brew your compost tea with chlorinated water, you'll kill many of the organisms you're trying to grow. The simple solution: fill your bucket with tap water and let it sit for 24 hours before using it. Chlorine is volatile and will off-gas at room temperature. Rainwater is even better — that's what I use when I have it.

**A bucket or container.** Five gallons is a practical size for home use. Food-grade plastic, a clean metal bucket, or even a glass vessel all work.

**An aquarium pump and air stone.** The aeration is what makes this work. You're not just soaking compost — you're actively pumping oxygen through the water, which creates conditions for aerobic organisms to multiply rapidly. A basic aquarium pump and bubble stone from any pet store will do the job. You want strong bubbling throughout the container.

**A mesh bag or old pillowcase.** Put your compost inside so you can remove it cleanly when the brew is done.

**Optional: a catalyst.** A small amount of unsulfured molasses — about a teaspoon per gallon — gives the bacteria an additional food source during brewing and can increase the final population of organisms. Some growers swear by it; others skip it. I use it when I have it.

## The Process, Step by Step

Step one: fill your bucket with unchlorinated water (or let it dechlorinate for 24 hours if using tap water).

Step two: fill your mesh bag with about two cups of finished compost per five gallons of water. Suspend it in the bucket.

Step three: submerge your air stone, connect the tubing to your pump, and start the aeration. You want vigorous bubbling — the water should be actively churning.

Step four: if using molasses, add it now and stir briefly.

Step five: let it brew for 24 hours. Not much longer — after about 24 to 36 hours, the oxygen can deplete even with aeration, and the population shifts toward anaerobic organisms you don't want.

Step six: remove the compost bag and apply the tea immediately. Compost tea is perishable — the organisms you've grown will start dying without the aeration. Use it within a few hours of finishing the brew.

## How to Apply It

You can apply compost tea two ways: as a soil drench or as a foliar spray.

As a soil drench: water it directly into your garden beds the way you'd water normally. Focus on the root zone of your plants. The organisms will establish in the soil and begin doing the work of the soil food web — cycling nutrients, building aggregates, suppressing pathogens.

As a foliar spray: pour the tea into a clean spray bottle or backpack sprayer and mist your plant leaves. Beneficial bacteria and fungi colonizing the leaf surfaces can help suppress foliar diseases. Spray in the early morning or evening — midday sun can kill the organisms before they can establish.

For a home garden, applying compost tea every two to four weeks during the growing season is typically sufficient. You're supplementing an existing biology, not replacing it.

## What to Expect (And What Not To Expect)

Compost tea is not a quick fix. You won't see your plants transform in three days. What you're building is infrastructure — soil biology that takes weeks and months to reach its full expression.

Over a season of regular application alongside good organic practices, you'll typically see improved soil structure (more aggregates, better water infiltration), healthier root systems, better resistance to environmental stress, and gradually increasing plant vigor. It's a compounding return.

The one realistic expectation I'd set is disease suppression. If you're dealing with fungal issues in your garden — powdery mildew, damping off, root rot — applying compost tea as a foliar spray and soil drench can make a meaningful difference within a few weeks. The biology is actively competing with those pathogens.

Y'all, compost tea is one of those practices where the return on investment is almost entirely in the biology you're growing. Give it a season and watch what happens.

---

## Sources

- [Living Soil, Healthy Garden — University of Minnesota Extension](https://extension.umn.edu/managing-soil-and-nutrients/living-soil-healthy-garden)
- [Compost and Soil Organic Matter — University of Minnesota Extension](https://extension.umn.edu/yard-and-garden-news/compost-and-soil-organic-matter-more-merrier)
- [Compost Research Possibilities — Cornell Composting](https://compost.css.cornell.edu/ResearchIdeas.html)
- [Compost: How to Make It and How Much to Use — Penn State Extension](https://extension.psu.edu/compost-how-to-make-it-and-how-much-to-use)
- [Composting At Home — US EPA](https://www.epa.gov/recycle/composting-home)

sources.title: Compost and Soil Organic Matter: The More, the Merrier?
Compost: How to Make It and How Much to Use

scottyQuotes: Compost tea is not liquid fertilizer. It's not nutrients. It's biology. And there's a meaningful difference between those two things.
The most important ingredient is unchlorinated water. Chlorine is there to kill bacteria — and that's exactly what it will do to your compost tea.
You're not building results in three days. You're building infrastructure. Give it a season and watch what happens.
